front: Song: Tevye sings 'all day long I'd biddy biddy bum' and imagines having a staircase going nowhere 'just for show'
back: "If I Were a Rich Man" (Fiddler on the Roof)
work: Songs: "If I Were a Rich Man"

front: Song: The nonsense words in this song were meant to evoke Hasidic chanting; performers often ignore the scripted onomatopoeias and imitate animal calls instead
back: "If I Were a Rich Man" (Fiddler on the Roof)
work: Songs: "If I Were a Rich Man"

front: Song: The singer asks if the title situation would 'spoil some vast eternal plan' and imagines 'a proper double chin' on his wife
back: "If I Were a Rich Man" (Fiddler on the Roof)
work: Songs: "If I Were a Rich Man"

front: Song: The opening number of Fiddler on the Roof, in which characters argue about whether a man delivered a horse or a mule
Song: An emotional wedding song in which characters ask 'is there a canopy in store for me?'
Song: After twenty-five years of marriage, Tevye and Golde answer the title question of this duet and conclude 'it's nice to know'
back: "Do You Love Me?" (Fiddler on the Roof)

front: Song: Motel the tailor exclaims this phrase when the butcher Lazar Wolf's marriage proposal to Tzeitel is rejected
Musical: Dancers balance bottles on their heads/hats to slowly accelerating music at a wedding in this show; Jerome Robbins visited Orthodox Jewish weddings for inspiration
back: Fiddler on the Roof (the Bottle Dance)

front: Musical: Tevye recounts a dream about Golde's grandmother that is interrupted by the appearance of this ghost
Musical: This actor originated Tevye on Broadway; his other credits include Pseudolus in A Funny Thing and Max Bialystock in The Producers
Musical: This Israeli actor played Tevye in two West End productions and the 1971 film; also played Milos Columbo in For Your Eyes Only
Musical: This Welsh bass-baritone played Tevye in a 2015 West End revival and at the BBC Proms
Musical: John Williams won his first Oscar for adapting the score of this show for a 1971 film that featured violin solos by Isaac Stern
Musical: Marc Chagall's paintings of a green-faced fiddler in his childhood shtetl inspired this musical's title
Musical: The family is eventually expelled from this town after a pogrom, ending the show
